item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Diego and Frank's poverty alleviation, and the CPS question, were very effective "Save the Kitten" moments that got me to care about this character. Excellent writing; I learned something about writing from reading this article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it is a typo, because the drag coefficient that I know of is kg/s, from the drag force F=- k v, where v is velocity.

Anyway, I liked the idea and examples of classes a lot! I will look for more skips from the author!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also would have accepted "Assuming everything's fine and good and normal, what's 2 + 2?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm thinking John's fatal error was the phrase "as much energy as possible." Shoulda been "as much energy as necessary to ensure his safe arrival."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I'm actually on my way to being a physics teacher (hopefully by Fall of 2020) myself. I made this one because it is something personal to me and I wanted to put a restraint that ensured simplicity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic! And as a math teacher I would totally write problems like this even if they weren't anomalously becoming reality. Great skip.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What do "k=0.38 kg/m" and "k=0.24 kg/m" mean?They seem to be something related to gravity or mass, but kg/m doesn't look like the correct unit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting concept escalated and executed well. I like how you showed the brutality the GOC exhibits to civilians despite its "international peacekeeper" role. The ending was a satisfying and clean resolution to the whole thing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've already expressed my specific executional concerns with this on IRC, and with this most recent update, I find myself thoroughly enjoying every word of this. Very good stuff, and the idea of retrograde storytelling like this is really cool to see.

Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great read. As I was going down the pages I enjoyed each note more and more
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the input! I was considering editing the special containment procedures to include having Foundation agents stay on alert in Calhoun County when alterations are made to the texts to ensure the expected effect takes place.

I actually cut a number of questions during critique as I worried it wasn't cutting to the chase. Things where it was more apparent that the teacher was becoming aware of his power and using it to get his students athletic scholarships and enrolled in MIT. The lightning strike question was supposed to be his experiment to confirm his powers are real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see what's going on and I've enjoyed until I reached the conclusion. I'm happy to upvote.

But I think some of the questions are too random, considering the main issue the guy was having…
(spoiler)

+ show block
– hide block

It seems the main issue is GOC killed his student so John destroyed their building, and attempted to travel back in time… what does Nancy and Riemann hypothesis have to do with that?

While I can guess former was how he found out the anomaly, and the latter was how GOC found out, I can't be certain. and Agent tucker is even more random, with no justification I can come up with..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description does a nice job of setting up what the SCP is about, but you really need to make things more special in your containment procedures. I feel like you escalated to violent tension in the logs too quickly.

The joy in something like this should come in the slow buildup, as you can imagine the student frowning at some early questions which are odd, but not garishly so, and then their mounting horror and confusion as we get to throwing people through guard rails and all the other bits. A simple re-ordering of your current logs would probably make this article much more enjoyable to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is awesome. We really need more math based SCPs on the site. I read this in the draft, and it’s nice to see it on the site. I love the subtle descent into madness that the article takes. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to add my own problems there too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not gonna lie, I don’t exactly know what made me like this. It doesn’t have most of the things I usually consider to be ‘fantastic’ but it absolutely doesn’t have anything that makes me want to downvote either. Maybe it’s the idea of having the various events in the problems play out that are a bit humorous, or maybe the all to common encroaching story in a list that’s mixed in with it, or maybe it’s just the simplicity of it. I don’t know. But I do know it’ll have my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope this one survives 'cause I like it. It looks like the positive votes are edging out the negative ones, so fingers crossed I guess.

Uh… Never mind, it appears that I reverse jinxed this. Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Some of the critters thought it was too exposition-y. That and I wanted the focus to be telling the story through the questions themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I left in SCP-XXXX for like 20 minutes by accident. Some people are fast on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually really like this. Nice job, weird that it slipped to -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I think you should have left in the teacher's notes to give some more context for what's going on though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my second skip. Wanted to try something that I feel is a bit more authentic to myself. Thanks to MaliceAforethought, rattles, TheBlueHour and RockTeethMothEyes for lending their critiques!

Sorry about the starting goof with the SCP-XXXX's left in. Fixed.

Edit 2: small correction in description. A4 and US letter sized papers are different sizes.
